headline: Nvidia stock performance a sign of changing AI market, says investor
summary: STORY: "There's a reason why Nvidia hasn't been doing very well, performing very well. When it reported earnings, all the numbers looked really great in terms of reports, but the stock fell, right? That, to me, tells me that the AI industry is expanding. It is refining itself and that means competitors are coming in. So I think picks and shovels as a large theme is still valid in AI, but I think you have to be careful because the winners are going to be coming from somewhere that you don't anticipate, and yesterday's winners may not be tomorrow's winners," said Rathbun.She added that investors should look at manufacturing and construction companies working on building data centers.

summary: STORY: The world's largest contract chipmaker TSMC says it's confident in its growth over the next few years...thanks to robust demand for computing power and advanced semiconductors as it rides a relentless AI boom.The Taiwanese firm's CEO spoke at its annual shareholders meeting on Thursday.C.C. Wei said customers are still positive on the outlook for AI, although the company continues to monitor the impact of rising component costs.Asked whether TSMC could raise prices for customers, Wei said the firm would like to do that as, quote, "we still need to make money."Taiwan plays a crucial role in the global AI supply chain for companies including Nvidia  and Apple.And its position is anchored by TSMC.The tech giant said it was working hard to meet all customer demands...although added that it will take a "very long time" to fully satisfy American customers' needs with production in the U.S.Looking ahead, Wei said he views autonomous vehicles as long-term growth drivers.But TSMC faces political risk as China steps up military pressure on democratically and separately governed Taiwan...which Beijing views as Chinese territory.

headline: AI Weekly: Jensen Huang's star turn, Anthropic moves to go public
summary: STORY: From Jensen Huang's star turn at Computex to Anthropic's move to go public...This is AI Weekly. :: AI WeeklyNvidia CEO Jensen Huang headlined Computex in Taiwan.He showed off Nvidia's new Vera central processing unit - or CPU.And he unveiled the RTX Spark PC chip, which brings AI capabilities directly into laptops and desktop computers.“There is no question this reinvention of the computer is as big of a deal as the reinvention of the phone into what we now know as the smartphone. And so this is the beginning of that journey."The Nvidia boss was a big draw at Computex, and dined with executives from the likes of Samsung and SK Hynix.Dell shares rocketed higher after the computer maker lifted revenue and profit expectations.The U.S. giant scored big from soaring demand for AI-optimized servers used in data centers.Dell also heavily raised its annual revenue forecast up to $169 billion.It's benefiting as tech giants like Alphabet and Amazon aim to spend over $700 billion on AI-related infrastructure this year.AI giant Anthropic said it has filed paperwork to go public.It gives it a lead over rival and ChatGPT owner OpenAI in a race that could define the future business shape of AI.The Claude maker didn't reveal the size or the terms of the offering. Its last fundraising round gave it a valuation of nearly $1 trillion, making it worth more than OpenAI.Broadcom shares plunged as much as 13% after its latest results.Investors were disappointed by the U.S. firm's outlook for demand, while second-quarter revenue also fell short of Wall Street hopes.Broadcom said it expects AI chip revenue of $16 billion in the third quarter - just below expectations.But CEO Hock Tan said the firm's long-range forecast for AI chip sales was unchanged at $100 billion.And Google parent Alphabet said it's looking to raise $80 billion in equity offerings.It wants the money to fund a costly expansion of its AI infrastructure.That includes a $10 billion investment from Berkshire Hathaway.The deal positions Warren Buffett's company as a major investor, and adds a high-profile endorsement of Alphabet's long-term AI and cloud strategy.

headline: TSMC boss upbeat on outlook as AI boom shows no sign of easing
summary: Taiwan's TSMC, the world's largest contract chipmaker, said on ‌Thursday it is confident in its growth ‌over the next few years, driven by robust demand for ​computing power and advanced semiconductors, as it rides an AI boom.  Chief Executive C.C. Wei, speaking at TSMC's annual shareholders' meeting in the northern Taiwanese city ‌of Hsinchu, added ⁠that its customers continue to express a positive outlook for the AI industry.  Taiwan has taken centrestage this week as it hosts the annual Computex conference, where leaders of some of the world's most powerful tech ‌companies are gathering, with executives ​from the likes of Nvidia ​to Intel praising ​the island's central role in the ‌global supply chain.

headline: Quantinuum Prices IPO at $60 a Share. It’s Slated to Go Public Thursday.
summary: Quantinuum raised $1.68 billion in its upsized initial public offering late Wednesday, setting the stage for one of the most anticipated tech listings of the year.  The quantum computing company priced its upsized IPO of 28 million shares at $60 apiece.  Underwriters have been granted a 30-day option to sell an additional 4.2 million shares if demand is higher than expected, Quantinuum said.

headline: SpaceX stock market debut set to make Elon Musk a trillionaire
summary: SpaceX plans to raise up to $75bn (£56bn) when it goes public this month – putting Elon Musk on course to become the world's first trillionaire.  The space company is gearing up for what is expected to be the largest-ever stock market debut.  It says it will sell 555.6 million shares at $135 (£100) a piece in its initial public offering (IPO).
